questions.reason: Reason (R): This right is mentioned in Article 15(2) of the Constitution.
questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The freedom to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India is not a fundamental right in India.
questions.explanation: Article 15(2) of the Indian Constitution states, 'Nothing in this article shall affect the operation of any existing law or policy resolution in so far as such law or policy resolution authorises discrimination having for its object the promotion of the interests of any mechanically backward class of citizens or for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or in so far as such law imposes, for the purpose of securing the proper representation of any such backward class or classes in any electoral college or legislative assembly constituted in accordance with this Constitution, any requirement as to residence within the State or local area.' This implies that the freedom to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India is not a fundamental right. However, the reason provided is also correct as it explains the specific exemption for certain classes of citizens.

questions.reason: Reason (R): This right is mentioned in Article 23 of the Constitution.
questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The right against exploitation is a fundamental right in India.
questions.explanation: Article 23 of the Indian Constitution states, 'Traffic in human beings is prohibited and any person for the purpose of or in the knowledge that he will be compelled to labour against his will, under the threat of any penalty or by means of any coercion or other forms of compulsion, is deprived of his liberty shall, for the purposes of this article, be deemed to be sold or let on hire.' This implies that the right against exploitation is indeed a fundamental right in India.
